title: The Second Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sunday, March 8, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Second Sunday in Lent, the fourth Sunday of the liturgical year following Ash Wednesday (February 26, 2020). The RCL readings for this date would have included Genesis 12:1-4a (God's call to Abram), Psalm 121, Romans 4:1-5, 13-17, and John 3:1-17 (Nicodemus and Jesus's nighttime conversation). The congregation was in the heart of the Lenten season, midway between Ash Wednesday and Palm Sunday (April 5).

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of General Convention 2018 decisions, with ongoing discussions about church polity and mission. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert J. O'Neill (who served 2015-2023), was navigating normal diocesan operations. No major Anglican Communion or national Episcopal controversies were reaching a peak at this specific moment, though broader tensions around biblical interpretation and LGBTQ+ inclusion remained constant currents in the denomination.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early spring weather in early March with typical unpredictability. The Colorado legislature was in its 2020 session (running through May), with various bills under debate. At the state and local level, Colorado was not yet under emergency declarations, though awareness of COVID-19 was beginning to register in news coverage. The Denver metro area remained functionally normal on March 8, though some institutions were beginning to monitor the pandemic situation.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The COVID-19 pandemic was accelerating as the dominant national story: on March 6, the first COVID-19 death in the US was confirmed in Washington State, and cases were spreading across multiple states. The Super Tuesday primaries had occurred just four days earlier (March 3), with Joe Biden emerging as the clear Democratic frontrunner after winning ten states, effectively ending Bernie Sanders's campaign viability. The stock market had experienced significant volatility related to pandemic fears and oil price collapse. The Trump administration was still in the early stages of its pandemic response, with mixed messaging about severity.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Italy was in the midst of the early, catastrophic phase of COVID-19, with the country becoming a global hotspot just as this sermon was preached—by March 8, Italy had over 7,300 confirmed cases and 366 deaths, prompting increasingly strict lockdown measures. The global pandemic narrative was shifting from "contained outbreak" to "potential worldwide crisis." The Iran nuclear deal remained a point of international tension under the Trump administration.

CULTURAL MOMENT

America was at an inflection point of denial and dawning alarm. Most people on March 8 did not yet understand that normal life was about to end, though healthcare workers, international observers, and those following closely recognized the trajectory. The mood was anxious but not yet panicked; institutions and churches were still operating normally. St. Patrick's Day celebrations were being planned, spring break travel was being booked, and March Madness was one week away from beginning. The dominant cultural emotion was uncertainty mixed with a sense that "this probably won't affect us much"—a perception that would prove catastrophically wrong within two weeks. For an Episcopal congregation, there was no sense that in-person worship would become impossible within days.
